metadata.pr_title: Add nodes for basic string operations
metadata.pr_description:
String nodes, requested in RFC https://github.com/Kosinkadink/rfcs/blob/main/rfcs/0000-corenodes.md#string-nodes
Designed nodes:
1. Concatenate, and input is: 
- string_a, 
- string_b 
- separator
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/894c0afc-664f-49a2-a0de-ed60c2aacfc5)
2. Substring, and input is:
- string
- start
- end
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1ab814a6-f360-43cb-86be-ac61486c9a78)

3. Length, and input is
- string
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eb4f7413-2f59-4faf-aaa7-cc3b0fbf4322)

4. Case Converter, and input is
- string
- mode, with options are ["UPPERCASE", "lowercase", "Capitalize", "Title Case"]
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba1041fd-40f5-4af0-a52b-c0df3ae3d3e4)

5. Trim, and input is
- string
- mode, with options are ["BOTH", "LEFT", "RIGHT"]
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6489d7d8-ab78-4e9e-b172-b4f7e3cbacb2)

6. Replace, and input is
- string
- find
- replace
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/997c09d6-75f2-4e71-90d5-6d0f77cfcb09)

7. Contains, and input is
- string
- substring
- case_sensitive
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e66adb5b-02de-4ba8-b848-f9f83215e694)

8. Compare, and input is
- string_a
- string_b
- mode, with options are ["StartsWith", "EndsWith", "Equal"]
- case_sensitive
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4dd3b9ae-f3d8-41bc-90c2-8e459a923840)

9. Regex Match, and input is
- string
- regex_pattern
- case_insensitive
- multiline
- dotall
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a57ea778-009b-4b8e-94d0-e440c6ca235d)

10. Regex Extract, and input is
- string
- regex_pattern
- mode, with options are ["First Match", "All Matches", "First Group", "All Groups"]
- case_insensitive
- multiline
- dotall
- group_index
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b4d1fce8-f2a9-422d-8b62-9ac90d50060a)

review_planner.summary: The changes introduce a new file `comfy_extras/nodes_string.py` containing various string manipulation classes and modifies `nodes.py` to include this new file in the list of extra nodes.

tasks.title: Security Review
tasks.description: Verify that there are no security vulnerabilities in the new string manipulation classes, particularly focusing on the regex operations in `RegexMatch` and `RegexExtract`.

tasks.title: Logic Review
tasks.description: Check the logic of all methods in the new string manipulation classes to ensure they behave as expected, especially edge cases like empty strings, special characters, and large inputs.

tasks.title: Performance Review
tasks.description: Assess the performance implications of the new string manipulation classes, particularly the regex operations which could be computationally expensive with large inputs or complex patterns.

tasks.title: General Review
tasks.description: Review the overall structure and consistency of the new string manipulation classes, ensuring they follow the existing coding standards and conventions. Also, check if any meaningful tests are missing.

general.summary: The new string manipulation classes in `comfy_extras/nodes_string.py` seem to follow existing coding standards and conventions. However, there are some areas that could benefit from additional scrutiny and testing.

logic.summary: The new string manipulation classes in `comfy_extras/nodes_string.py` introduce various string operations such as concatenation, substring extraction, length calculation, case conversion, trimming, replacement, containment checks, comparison, regex matching, and regex extraction. The review focuses on potential issues related to edge cases, error handling, and missing tests.

performance.summary: The new string manipulation classes, especially the regex operations, introduce potential performance concerns due to the computational expense associated with large inputs or complex patterns.

security.summary: No high-severity security issues found in the added string manipulation classes. However, there are some potential areas to review further.
warnings: No explicit tests were found for the new string manipulation classes, including `RegexMatch` and `RegexExtract`. Consider adding comprehensive tests to ensure correct functionality and prevent future regressions.

reflection_reports.rationale: The `RegexMatch` class handles regex patterns but does not validate the input pattern for potential ReDoS attacks. This is a valid security concern.

The issue is real but not primarily security. The `RegexMatch` class uses `re.search()` to perform regex matching. It handles regex errors gracefully but does not validate the `regex_pattern` input, which could lead to unexpected behavior or errors if an invalid pattern is provided. This is more of a logic issue than a security issue.

The `RegexMatch` class uses `re.search` to match a regex pattern against a string. It handles exceptions from `re.error` gracefully by returning `False`. However, there is no input sanitization or validation for the `regex_pattern`, which could lead to ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) attacks if malicious patterns are provided. This is a valid security concern.

The `RegexExtract` class also uses various regex functions (`re.search`, `re.findall`, `re.finditer`) without validating the `regex_pattern`. Similar to `RegexMatch`, this could lead to ReDoS attacks if malicious patterns are provided. This is a valid security concern.
The `StringSubstring` class does not handle cases where `start` or `end` indices are out of bounds, which could lead to unexpected behavior or errors.
The `StringLength` class correctly calculates the length of the string but does not handle the case of `None` being passed as input, which could lead to a `TypeError`.

The `CaseConverter` class handles different modes for case conversion but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning the original string without any transformation.

The `StringTrim` class handles different modes for trimming but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning the original string without any trimming.
The `StringReplace` class uses the `str.replace()` method, which is efficient but does not handle cases where `find` is an empty string, which could lead to an infinite loop.
The `StringContains` class correctly checks for the presence of a substring but does not handle cases where either `string` or `substring` is `None`, which could lead to a `TypeError`.

The `StringCompare` class handles different modes for string comparison but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning `False` without any comparison.

The `RegexExtract` class handles different modes for regex extraction but does not validate the `mode` or `group_index` parameters. If an invalid mode or group index is provided, it defaults to returning an empty string without any extraction.
The `StringContains` class converts strings to lowercase for case-insensitive comparison, which can be inefficient for large strings due to the overhead of creating new string objects.

The `RegexMatch` and `RegexExtract` classes use regex operations (`re.search`, `re.findall`, and `re.finditer`) that can be computationally expensive with large inputs or complex patterns, posing a potential performance risk.

The `NODE_CLASS_MAPPINGS` dictionary includes all defined classes, but there are no tests to verify their functionality. This is a maintainability issue as comprehensive tests are essential for ensuring the correctness and reliability of the implemented string manipulation classes.

While the candidate points out the lack of comprehensive test cases, which is a maintainability concern, the severity and nature of the issue align more closely with logic and correctness. The absence of tests can lead to undetected bugs and incorrect behavior, especially for edge cases.

candidate_findings.content:
NODE_CLASS_MAPPINGS = {
    "StringConcatenate": StringConcatenate,
    "StringSubstring": StringSubstring,
    "StringLength": StringLength,
    "CaseConverter": CaseConverter,
    "StringTrim": StringTrim,
    "StringReplace": StringReplace,
    "StringContains": StringContains,
    "StringCompare": StringCompare,
    "RegexMatch": RegexMatch,
    "RegexExtract": RegexExtract
}
candidate_findings.evidence_summary: The `NODE_CLASS_MAPPINGS` dictionary includes all defined classes but lacks tests to verify their functionality.
The `RegexMatch` class handles regex patterns but does not validate the input pattern for potential ReDoS attacks.
The `StringContains` class converts strings to lowercase for case-insensitive comparison, which might be inefficient for large strings.

        return string[start:end],
candidate_findings.evidence_summary: The `StringSubstring` class does not handle cases where `start` or `end` indices are out of bounds. This could lead to unexpected behavior or errors.
The `StringLength` class correctly calculates the length of the string. However, it does not handle the case of `None` being passed as input, which could lead to a `TypeError`.

The `CaseConverter` class handles different modes for case conversion but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning the original string without any transformation.

The `StringTrim` class handles different modes for trimming but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning the original string without any trimming.
The `StringReplace` class uses the `str.replace()` method, which is efficient but does not handle cases where `find` is an empty string, which could lead to an infinite loop.
The `StringContains` class correctly checks for the presence of a substring but does not handle cases where either `string` or `substring` is `None`, which could lead to a `TypeError`.

The `StringCompare` class handles different modes for string comparison but does not validate the `mode` parameter. If an invalid mode is provided, it defaults to returning `False` without any comparison.

The `RegexMatch` class uses `re.search()` to perform regex matching. It handles regex errors gracefully but does not validate the `regex_pattern` input, which could lead to unexpected behavior or errors if an invalid pattern is provided.

The `RegexExtract` class handles different modes for regex extraction but does not validate the `mode` or `group_index` parameters. If an invalid mode or group index is provided, it defaults to returning an empty string without any extraction.

candidate_findings.content: All methods lack comprehensive test cases covering edge cases like empty strings, special characters, and large inputs.
candidate_findings.evidence_summary: The diff does not include any test cases for the new string manipulation classes. Comprehensive testing is essential to ensure correct behavior across various scenarios.

candidate_findings.content: The new string manipulation classes, especially `RegexMatch` and `RegexExtract`, involve regex operations which can be computationally expensive with large inputs or complex patterns.
candidate_findings.evidence_summary: The `RegexMatch` and `RegexExtract` classes use `re.search`, `re.findall`, and `re.finditer` for regex operations. These functions can have significant performance implications depending on the input size and complexity of the regex pattern.

The `RegexMatch` class uses `re.search` to match a regex pattern against a string. It handles exceptions from `re.error` gracefully by returning `False`. However, there is no input sanitization or validation for the `regex_pattern`, which could lead to ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) attacks if malicious patterns are provided.

The `RegexExtract` class also uses various regex functions (`re.search`, `re.findall`, `re.finditer`) without validating the `regex_pattern`. Similar to `RegexMatch`, this could lead to ReDoS attacks if malicious patterns are provided.
